data.name: Mohamed El Shenawy
data.description: El-Shenawy was forged in the lower tiers of Egyptian football before finding his way to the top. He joined Al Ahly's youth system as a teenager and spent his formative years there before leaving in 2009, first to Tala'ea El Gaish, then Haras El Hodoud, and later Petrojet, where he spent three seasons honing his craft. He returned to Al Ahly in 2016 and has been the club's first-choice goalkeeper for most of that time, racking up over 350 appearances in all competitions. Internationally, he was Egypt's No 1 at the 2018 World Cup in Russia, the Pharaohs' first appearance in 28 years, and has kept 40 clean sheets in 75 caps for his country at the time of writing. After winning man of the match for Egypt against South Africa at the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations, he said: “This award isn’t just for me. It is for the 28 players in the squad. We play as the heart of one man for one nation,” before adding that the goalkeepers' union is “all one. We play for Egypt together and represent 120 million people.” At 1.91m (6ft 3in), he is known for his imposing aerial presence and shot-stopping reflexes, but can be error-prone. Now 37, he faces a battle against the younger Shobeir to start at this World Cup, but his experience and stature in Egyptian football make him an invaluable member of the squad.

data.name: Yasser Ibrahim
data.description: Ibrahim made a name for himself at Smouha, where he spent four seasons before joining Al Ahly in 2019 and becoming one of their most dependable defensive pillars at the club, making more than 230 appearances. He has a knack of popping up with the odd important goal too, making international headlines in 2021 after a first-half double against Saudi Arabia’s Al Hilal in the Club World Cup and scoring Egypt’s winner in extra time against Benin in the last-16 at the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations with a looping header. At 33, he is among the most experienced defenders in the squad and his international career has seen a late bloom, with just 16 senior caps to his name at the time of writing.

data.name: Mohamed Hany
data.description: Hany has spent his entire career at Al Ahly, where he has become one of the most decorated right-backs in African club football. He was promoted to the first team in 2014-15 and has since made more than 390 senior appearances for Al Ahly, winning eight league titles, four African Champions League trophies, and six Egyptian Super Cups. Hany’s playing style is defined by his pace and stamina that allows him to operate as an attacking threat down the right flank. He has 40 caps for the national team at the time of writing and has been a regular participant in Al Ahly's memorable continental campaigns.

data.name: Hossam Abdelmaguid
data.description: One of the most highly rated young defenders in Egyptian football, Abdelmaguid joined Zamalek's system as a teenager and has become one of the club's most important players. He won his first league title in 2020-21 and added a second the following season, then starred as the club won the African Confederation Cup and the African Super Cup in 2024. Abdelmaguid – at 1.94m (6ft 4.5in) – is a dominant aerial presence who can play out from the back. On competition within the team, he said: “I always work and do what I have to do. Who will play and who will not play is up to the coach. What I have to do is to kill myself on the field and whatever the coach wants me to do, that's it. It’s done.” Still only 24, he is considered one of Egypt's best defensive prospects for the next decade. His contract with Zamalek ends in the summer and according to his agent and brother, Abdelmaguid has had interest from West Brom and Anderlecht among other European clubs.

data.name: Ramy Rabia
data.description: Ramy Rabia joined Al Ahly's academy and made his first-team debut at just 17 back in 2010, quickly establishing himself as one of the brightest young defenders in Egyptian football. During his two stints at Al Ahly – interrupted by a short and largely unsuccessful spell at Sporting in between – he won 10 Egyptian Premier League titles, six Afrtican Champions League trophies, and four African Super Cups. Rabia, a seasoned international with more than 40 caps for Egypt, has seen fluctuating involvement with the national team over the years but 2025 marked his most active year for his country, playing the most matches in a single calendar year under the national team coach, Hossam Hassan. The 32-year-old is a vocal leader with physical strength and composure in high-pressure moments.

data.name: Mohamed Abdelmonem
data.description: Abdelmonem is one of Egypt’s most promising young defenders but he wasn’t always destined to play as centre-back. “I started off as an attacker,” he said. “When I was 10, my club was at a tournament, facing a team with a really strong No 9. He was absolutely destroying our defenders, hitting and pushing them. My coach told me to go back and play man-to-man against him … I played a really good game against him and from then on, the coach saw me as a defender. I would call my dad, crying, telling him that I wanted to play as a No 10 … Now, I try to be the best defender I can be, while also scoring goals.” Abdelmonem joined the Ligue 1 side Nice from Al Ahly in 2024, becoming the first Egyptian player to represent the southern French club. The 27-year-old spent his formative years at Al Ahly, clocking 115 appearances across three seasons and winning 10 major honours. He gained further recognition outside of the continent after stellar performances during Egypt’s run to the 2021 Africa Cup of Nations final. However, eight months into his Nice career, he ruptured an anterior cruciate ligment and has only just made his way into first-team training.

data.name: Trézéguet
data.description: Trezeguet's nickname, borrowed from the French football legend David Trezeguet, has been with him since his teenage years and has defined a career as colourful as any Egyptian footballer's. He broke into Al Ahly's first team at 18, helping them win back-to-back African Champions League titles in 2012 and 2013. He made his biggest European impact at Kasimpasa in Turkey, becoming the seventh Egyptian to reach double figures in a European league and setting a new record for goals by an Egyptian in the Turkish top flight. Aston Villa signed him in 2019 where his goals helped them avoid the drop in 2020. He later returned to Turkey before moving back to Al Ahly. He has over 90 caps and more than 20 goals for Egypt and his contributions to the 2018 World Cup qualifying campaign established his legacy in Egyptian football history. “I’ve played in the best league in the world, the Premier League. I’ve played in front of incredible crowds in Turkey. I would give those all up if I needed to,” Trezeguet said. “I would never give up representing Egypt.” Now, at 31, he arrives at this World Cup as one of the squad's most experienced players and as a player whom many of his teammates grew up watching.

data.name: Emam Ashour
data.description: Ashour hails from a football family and as a child, he trained with the older age groups at Ghazl El Mahalla and nearly quit the game when youth coaches overlooked him. “I played at El Mahalla only because my brothers played there,” he says. Ashour eventually earned promotion to El Mahalla’s first team and soon became one of Egypt’s most eye-catching talents. In 2019, he signed for Zamalek, where he helped the White Knights win domestic titles and forced his way into the national team, later even moving briefly to Midtjylland in Denmark, before joining his former team’s bitter rivals Al Ahly in 2023. There, Ashour’s flair and scoring really took him to the upper echelons of domestic football and the midfielder became the league’s top scorer in 2024-25. Injuries, however, have been recurring after a broken collarbone cut short his Club World Cup in 2025. Ashour is deployed as an attacking No 10 for Egypt, roaming between the lines, creating chances, and typically partnering other playmakers in a free role.

data.name: Hamza Abdelkarim
data.description: The youngest player in this group by some distance, and one of the most exciting prospects in Egyptian football. He came through Al Ahly’s academy, played for Egypt at under-17 level, and then joined Barcelona B during the winter transfer window on loan. On 23 June Barcelona announced that they had signed the forward on a three-year deal. Abdelkarim, the first Egyptian player in the Catalan club’s history, had already made a name for himself with their Under-19 A side, scoring five goals in seven league matches. His senior Egypt call-up this summer is clearly a gamble, but not a wild one. Hossam Hassan has left out more established forwards and kept a teenage striker who has barely started his senior career, but the attraction is obvious: Hamza, known as the Egyptian Haaland, is tall, mobile, and a natural penalty-area player in a squad that otherwise leans heavily on wide forwards and midfield runners for goals. He is unlikely to be asked to carry Egypt’s attack, but if the side need a late body in the box, the youngest player in the squad may suddenly become extremely important.

data.name: Mohamed Salah
data.description: The Egyptian King needs little introduction, and his career has become a permanent argument against ceilings. In December, he became the player with the most goal involvements at a single club in Premier League history, with 277, surpassing Wayne Rooney. No one has won more Golden Boot awards in the league’s history than his four (joint-top with Thierry Henry), and he is the highest scoring foreign player in the league. Salah has led Egypt to two Africa Cup of Nations finals, in 2017 and 2021, and he was named in the team of the tournament on each run. His defining national-team moment, however, remains the stoppage time penalty against Congo in October 2017 that sent Egypt to the 2018 World Cup and ended a 28-year absence from the competition. Since announcing that he will call time on his storied nine-year spell at Liverpool, he has played with a certain freedom, despite not being at his best this season. Salah has scored 65 international goals at the time of writing, second on his country’s all-time list and chasing the record of 69 held by Hossam Hassan, Egypt's head coach. As captain and the focal point of the squad, he remains the player around whom successive coaches have built their plans.

data.name: Mostafa Ziko
data.description: Mostafa Mohamed Zaki Abdelraouf – AKA Ziko – has taken the long road into the national team. From Shibin El-Kom he worked his way through Haras El Hodoud, Zed and Pyramids to take his place among Egypt’s elite. His proud mother says his rise came after “years of effort and challenges”, adding that he had to take responsibility too early in life when his father died when Ziko was just 11. His Egypt debut could hardly have gone better. In the warm-up friendly against Russia in Cairo he came on and scored the only goal of the match with a second-half header. Afterwards he said: “Even if I had scripted the scenario myself, I could not have imagined my debut going this way.” Ziko is not expected to be a starter this summer – Egypt’s positions up front are crowded, with Mohamed Salah, Omar Marmoush, Trezeguet, Ibrahim Adel and others ahead of him. But his value may be as a bench player who can cover several attacking roles and arrive without the burden of expectation carried by the bigger names.

data.name: Haissem Hassan
data.description: Haissem Hassan's international eligibility story is a tale of multiple competing claims. Born in Paris to an Egyptian father and Tunisian mother, and having represented France at youth level, he was sought by two national federations. Tunisia confirmed they had approached him; Egypt also made contact. Hassan made his decision on 15 March 2026 to represent Egypt, expressing his desire to compete at the World Cup. He launched his professional career with Châteauroux in France at just 16 in 2018, then moved to Villarreal in 2020, spending time in their reserve ranks before loan spells at Mirandés and Sporting Gijón. In August 2024, he joined Real Oviedo in Spain's second division, before being promoted to La Liga, and has become a regular starter since. The 24-year-old is predominantly a right-sided attacker, quick, direct, and comfortable in one-v-one situations.

data.name: Ahmed Fatouh
data.description: A product of Zamalek's youth academy, Fatouh was a vital part of the side that won back-to-back league titles in 2021 and 2022. His crossing ability and technical skill have made him a favorite of successive Zamalek and Egypt national team coaches but in August 2024 he received a one-year suspended prison sentence, a 20,000 EGP fine (£275) and a revoked licence after a fatal car accident killed a 55-year-old police officer. Examination confirmed he was driving under the influence of drugs. He also agreed to pay the victim's family 12m EGP for restitution. Addressing rumours that Zamalek had covered the payment, Fatouh said: "I paid the entire compensation myself. It was my mistake.” The case left his career in limbo for months and he is attempting to rebuild his reputation and form.

data.name: Hamdy Fathy
data.description: A combative defensive midfielder whose career has taken him from the trenches of Egyptian football to continental glory with Al Ahly. Fathy began his professional career with Damanhour, moved to Enppi in 2015, then to Petrojet one year later, and joined Al Ahly in 2019. At Al Ahly, his career truly flourished, quickly establishing himself as a regular starter and achieved impressive success, winning three each of the Egyptian Premier League, the Egyptian Cup, the Egyptian Super Cup and the African Champions League. He then moved to the Qatari side Al-Wakrah in 2023. In the summer of 2025, Al Ahly brought him back on loan specifically to participate in the Club World Cup. Fathy is also a national-team regular, with 62 appearances to his name at the time of writing, playing as a disruptor in front of the defence. “I can play as a No 6, as a centre-back in a three, in a two. Whatever the coach wants, I will do,” he says. “I have no preference. What matters to me is only the best interests of the team.”

data.name: Karim Hafez
data.description: Hafez left Egypt young, making his way through Belgium, Cyprus, France and Turkey, and was often linked with moves back home. Several years ago, when asked about Al Ahly and Zamalek, he said that he didn't intend to return to Egypt – but football, as usual, had other plans. He made the move home in 2022, joined Pyramids, and grew into a hugely consistent, experienced option on the left. His international career has been intermittent. Hafez spent four years outside the squad before returning in May’s friendly win over Russia. He’s not the quickest left-back in the squad, but he gives Egypt balance, a natural left foot and cover in a position where Hossam Hassan has few perfect solutions. He can be profound on social media, once writing: “We need to accept that we won’t always make the right decisions that we’ll screw up royally sometimes – understanding that failure is not the opposite of success. It’s part of success.”

data.name: El Mahdy Soliman
data.description: A seasoned veteran whose career path has threaded through much of Egyptian club football, Soliman is now the elder statesman of the goalkeeping corps. The 39-year-old began his professional career with Enppi in 2008 and went on to play for several other Egyptian clubs before joining Zamalek in 2025. Standing 1.89m (6ft 2.5in) tall, Soliman is a composed, experienced presence in goal, praised for his composure and his ability to play with his feet, traits that have seen him compared to modern sweeper‑keepers.

data.name: Mohanad Lasheen
data.description: Lasheen is a player who knows the value of hard work. He was a part of the Alassiouty (now Pyramids) squad that won promotion to the Egyptian Premier League, scoring the winning goal that guaranteed their ascent five weeks before the end of the season. He is a versatile central midfielder with a keen eye for a pass and a strong defensive work ethic. When Lasheen joined Egypt’s squad in 2025 during the World Cup qualifiers, he did so while battling injury. At the time, he said that from the start of the camp, the players understood the weight of the qualifiers and that he was “racing against time” to be fit. With 21 caps, he remains a vital part of the national team's midfield rotation and was deemed fit just in time for the World Cup.

data.name: Nabil Emad
data.description: Nabil Emad, better known as Dunga, came through the lower ranks of Egyptian football before moving to Pyramids, then Zamalek in 2022, where he became a defensive midfield mainstay. He won the Egyptian Premier League, the Egyptian Cup, the African Confederation Cup and the African Super Cup there before, in the last winter window, joining Saudi Arabia’s Al-Najma. His nickname has been with him a long time, ever since one of his junior coaches compared him to the Brazilian World-Cup-winning legend. For the national team, Dunga provides Hossam Hassan with a more conservative midfield option. While he may not be as flashy as Emam Ashour or as expansive as Marwan Attia, he will certainly be useful when Egypt need to win duels and second balls. His place this summer was thrown into doubt days after the squad was announced when he was the subject of allegations of rape made by an Italian woman. His lawyer, Shaaban Saeed, strongly denied the claims, saying his client was victim of "a campaign to distort the player's image".

data.name: Marwan Attia
data.description: Attia is a hard-working central midfielder known for his engine and passing. He came up through the ranks at Al Ittihad Alexandria, breaking into their first team in 2019. He spent four seasons as a steady presence and even captain before moving to Al Ahly. “I have been playing football since I was seven, and my forever dream … was to wear that red jersey,” Attia said. “When I knew Al Ahly wanted me, I didn’t think twice. I felt in my bones that I would be living my childhood dream.” He has emerged under Hossam Hassan as the foil to more attack-minded teams though he can also surge forward on occasion. At the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations, he burst into the box to score a brilliant opener against Benin, and later whipped in a cross for the winner. Off the field, Attia has shown quiet determination in tough times, losing his father in 2023, yet immediately returning to action to play a key role in securing a treble for Al Ahly.

data.name: Ibrahim Adel
data.description: A lightning-fast left winger, Adel emerged from the Port Said scene, scouted at the age of eight. The son of the former Zamalek forward Ashraf Adel, he inherited his family's love of the game. His explosive speed and eye for goal drew attention quickly and he earned a move to Pyramids in 2018, helping them win their first two trophies ever, the African Champions League and the Egyptian Cup with his swagger and long-range strikes earning him the nickname Baby Hulk. Getafe tried to sign him, even flying him to Spain, but Pyramids held firm. Adel later said: “The club wants to keep me… and I respect their decision.” Now on loan at the Danish club Nordsjælland, where their Egyptian sporting executive, Hossam El Zanati, said the club had followed him for more than three years and needed a “superstar” to open a pathway from Egypt. He was named best player at the 2023 Under-23 Africa Cup of Nations and has 21 senior international caps with three goals.

data.name: Mahmoud Saber
data.description: Saber, now with Zed, has taken a winding route through Egyptian football: from Nogoom to Pyramids, then loans and short spells with Zed and Smouha before returning to Zed again in 2025. At 24, he has already played for the senior national team 14 times despite making his debut only in 2024. Saber is known for his ability to find space in crowded midfields and his precise through balls. He is right-footed, tactically adaptable and can play as an attacking midfielder or off the left when required.

data.name: Omar Marmoush
data.description: In another life, Marmoush might have donned the red-and-white colors of a different team. “After about three years of being in Germany, Canada contacted me,” the forward has said. “They told me they knew I had citizenship and wanted me to join their setup. The coach called me personally. But [my decision] had already been made a long time ago. The Egyptian national team, to me, is above anything else.” At 17, he made his mark at Wadi Degla; then joined Wolfsburg, Stuttgart and St Pauli before his Eintracht Frankfurt performances secured a transfer to Manchester City. “Without him it would not be possible to qualify for the Champions League and reach the FA Cup final,” said his club manager, Pep Guardiola. “He’s a special player. I see him more as a striker not a proper winger.” Hossam Hassan has yet to follow Guardiola’s advice, often deploying Marmoush on the flank. Still, with 46 caps and 10 goals for the national team at the time of writing, his is one of the first two names on the team sheet, along with Salah's.

data.name: Mostafa Shobeir
data.description: Growing up, Shobeir could hardly have escaped football's gravity. His father, Ahmed Shobeir, is one of Egypt's most celebrated goalkeepers as the man between the sticks at the 1990 World Cup and at Al Ahly over a 13-year career. His son has followed in those footsteps, joining Al Ahly's academy before making his professional debut in 2020. Since then, he has established himself as a reliable presence for his club that is always calm under pressure. The 26-year-old kept nine consecutive clean sheets in the African Champions League – a competition record – on the way to his third continental trophy during the 2023-24 season and Fifa described him before the Club World Cup as "impregnable".

data.name: Tarek Alaa
data.description: One of the youngest members of the squad, Alaa is a versatile player who can operate at right-back or in the defensive midfield role with equal composure. Currently playing for Zed, on loan from Pyramids, he has made his mark as a technically confident player comfortable on the ball in tight spaces. Reports have suggested Al Ahly are keen to sign Alaa as an understudy and eventual replacement for Mohamed Hany. With just one international cap to his name at the time of writing, Alaa is still developing, and the World Cup represents an opportunity for one of Egyptian football's promising younger profiles to prove himself on the biggest stage.

data.name: Zizo
data.description: Zizo has carved out a reputation as one of Egyptian club football's most clinical, goalscoring wingers. His finest campaign came in 2021-22 with Zamalek, when he finished as the league’s top scorer with 19 goals. When his contract expired in June 2025, Al Ahly, Zamalek's fiercest rivals, moved swiftly to sign Zizo on a four-year deal, making it one of the most controversial transfers in Egyptian football. “How could Al Ahly not sign the best player in Egypt, especially since he was a free agent?” said the former Egyptian and Zamalek goalkeeper, Nader El-Sayed. “The Zizo deal wasn’t spiteful. He’s the best player in Africa, and he came on a free transfer. Al Ahly is similar to Real Madrid.” The 30-year-old brings pace, technique and an unerring eye for goal to Egypt's wide attacking options. “I always wanted to be a footballer,” he says. “I never thought about what level I could reach, didn’t even dream about playing for Egypt, but I was always going to be a footballer. There was no other option.”

data.name: Mohamed Alaa
data.description: The youngest of Egypt's four goalkeepers, Alaa is an intriguing wildcard selection. The 26-year-old has established himself as one of the more capable shot-stoppers outside the country's three traditional giants at El Gouna. Last season, Alaa was the main reason El Gouna survived relegation, keeping two clean sheets in three playoff matches. His inclusion in Egypt's squad for the first time during the March international break reflects Hossam Hassan’s willingness to look beyond the big-club establishments for reliable talent.
